retrieved_docs.title: Odontogenic Tumors of the Jaws -- History and Physical -- Ameloblastoma
retrieved_docs.content: Clinically, it appears as a small, well-circumscribed lesion (less than 3 cm) around an unerupted tooth. A calcifying epithelial odontogenic tumor (Pindborg tumor) is a rare tumor that accounts for less than 1% of the total number of odontogenic tumor cases. It mainly affects patients between the age of 30 and 50 years of age. However, fewer cases have been reported with a wider age range. [49] Mandible seems to be the most affected area, with around 66% of cases. [50]

retrieved_docs.title: Ameloblastoma -- Consultations
retrieved_docs.content: Consider consultation with a dentist, OMS, and/or ENT surgeon.

retrieved_docs.title: Ameloblastoma -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Ameloblastomas are rare, odontogenic tumors derived from odontogenic ectoderm. Ameloblastomas represent about 1% of all jaw tumors, but they are the second-most common odontogenic tumor. This activity outlines the evaluation and management of ameloblastoma and explains the role of the interprofessional team in evaluating and treating patients with this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: Odontogenic Tumors of the Jaws -- History and Physical -- Ameloblastoma
retrieved_docs.content: Other tumors include adenomatoid odontogenic tumors, an uncommon tumor representing 3% to 7% of all odontogenic tumors. Most cases are diagnosed in the second decade of life  and show female sex predilection. It often affects the anterior maxilla. [47] [48]

retrieved_docs.title: Ameloblastoma -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Describe the pathophysiology of ameloblastoma. Outline the typical presentation of a patient with malignant ameloblastoma. Identify the most common adverse events associated with malignant ameloblastoma. Explain the importance of collaboration and communication amongst the interprofessional team to ensure appropriate selection of candidates for malignant ameloblastoma surgery and to enhance postoperative management. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Odontogenic Tumors of the Jaws -- History and Physical -- Ameloblastoma
retrieved_docs.content: The most common clinical feature is painless, slowly growing mandibular mass. Squamous odontogenic tumor is also another rare benign odontogenic tumor that may be clinically aggressive. The affected patients are usually in their third decade of life. The most common presentation is a slowly growing painless swelling and loosening of teeth. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Odontogenic Cysts -- Evaluation -- Dentigerous Cyst
retrieved_docs.content: Dentigerous cysts are commonly associated with an impacted tooth (eruption delayed or partially erupted). It is essential to obtain radiographs to evaluate. Clinically, a dentigerous cyst is asymptomatic unless it is inflamed. Radiographically, dentigerous cysts appear as well-demarcated, unilocular radiolucency located at the cementoenamel junction of the tooth. They may appear radiographically similar to an OKC or ameloblastoma. [1] [2] It has been theorized that radiolucencies exceeding 4 mm indicate more aggressive behavior in which tooth displacement can occur.

retrieved_docs.title: Odontogenic Tumors of the Jaws -- Treatment / Management -- Ameloblastoma
retrieved_docs.content: Owing to the well-circumscribed nature of the adenomatoid odontogenic tumor lesion and it is being encapsulated, enucleation and curettage are considered to be curative in the majority of cases. [64] There is no consensus on the ideal treatment of calcifying epithelial odontogenic tumor (Pindborg tumor) cases. This is due to the small number of reported cases and lack of prolonged follow-up. Most surgeons will treat it like ameloblastoma with 1 cm bony linear margin resection and removing the clinically unaffected soft tissue layer as an anatomical barrier. This is due to the highly infiltrative and destructive capacity of these tumors. [50] Finally, for squamous odontogenic tumors cases, most surgeons will treat them by local excision. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Odontogenic Tumors of the Jaws -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The genetic mutation affects the cell cycle, especially the G1- S checkpoint. This stimulates the formation of odontogenic cysts or tumors. Studies suggest that mutations in ameloblastin, KRAS, FHIT, and P53 genes are related to the development of odontogenic tumors. [2] [3] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Odontogenic Tumors of the Jaws -- Evaluation -- Ameloblastoma
retrieved_docs.content: Other tumors include adenomatoid odontogenic tumor that usually appears as a well-circumscribed unilocular translucency around an unerupted tooth, usually a canine. [47] [48] A calcifying epithelial odontogenic tumor (Pindborg tumor) can be either unilocular or multi-locular. It usually appears as a radiolucent area with flecks of radio-opacities with an indistinct line of demarcation from the unaffected bone. It may be associated with an impacted tooth. [55] (Fig. 4) Finally, squamous odontogenic tumors that are usually unilocular in small lesions and multilocular in large ones and they may displace the affected tooth. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Ameloblastoma -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Ameloblastomas can occur over a broad age range, and most commonly affect patients between the ages of 20 to 40 years. They are uncommon in children younger than ten years. Males and females are equally affected. Ameloblastomas are located most commonly in the posterior mandible, with fewer tumors arising in the maxilla.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2181
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to sublingual gland is classified as a malignant tumor in the sublingual gland (ورم خبيث في الغدة تحت اللسان), indicating that this metastatic cancer represents a specific form of malignant neoplasm affecting the sublingual salivary gland. Recognizing this relationship clarifies the pathological nature and precise anatomical location of the disease, which is essential for accurate diagnosis, staging, and therapeutic planning in patients with salivary gland malignancies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2192
retrieved_docs.content: Primary malignant neoplasm of bone of the face is a specific type of neoplasm of bone of the face, representing a malignant tumor that originates in the facial bones. This relationship indicates that the broader category “neoplasm of bone of the face” encompasses the more specific entity “primary malignant neoplasm of bone of the face,” clarifying the hierarchical classification of facial bone tumors for diagnostic and therapeutic considerations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2179
retrieved_docs.content: Melanomas of skin are malignant neoplasms that arise from melanocytes in the cutaneous tissue and represent a specific form of skin cancer. سرطان الشفة الخبيث, which translates to malignant lip cancer, is a distinct malignant tumor that originates in the lip and is classified as an oral cavity cancer. Although both conditions are cancers, they affect different anatomical sites and are not subtypes of one another; each requires separate diagnostic and therapeutic approaches tailored to its location and biological behavior. Understanding the differences between melanomas of skin and سرطان الشفة الخبيث is important for accurate diagnosis, appropriate treatment planning, and effective patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2709
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to sublingual gland is identified as a specific form of malignant tumor in the sublingual gland, as indicated by its inverse_isa relationship to ورم خبيث في الغدة تحت اللسان. Both Metastatic malignant neoplasm to sublingual gland and the broader category Neoplasm, uncertain whether benign or malignant share the relationship termed النقيلة, suggesting a common classification or characteristic within neoplastic disease. Recognizing that the metastatic malignant neoplasm to the sublingual gland represents a subset of malignant sublingual gland tumors underscores its aggressive potential and the need for thorough evaluation, while the ambiguous neoplasm category highlights the importance of histopathological assessment to determine benign versus malignant status. The linking term النقيلة therefore serves as a unifying descriptor that aids clinicians in diagnosing, categorizing, and managing these related neoplastic conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 1 -- Community 248
retrieved_docs.content: inverse_isa of سرطان الرأس والعنق, highlighting its role as a specific manifestation of head‑and‑neck cancer. Together, these interrelated entities illustrate that malignant bone tumors of the skull and facial region are integrated within broader head and neck oncology, can arise as primary or metastatic lesions, and are represented across multiple linguistic and classification frameworks, which is essential for comprehensive diagnosis, coding, and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 72
retrieved_docs.content: The category “diseases of mouth; excluding dental” is classified as a subcategory of “اِضطِراب الغدة اللُّعابِيَّة” (salivary gland disorder), indicating that non‑dental oral diseases are considered part of the broader spectrum of salivary gland pathology. This relationship highlights the importance of evaluating salivary gland function when assessing oral conditions that are not of dental origin, thereby guiding more comprehensive diagnosis and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2168
retrieved_docs.content: Secondary Lesion is identified as a finding associated with سرطانة العظم, indicating that the presence of secondary lesions may support the diagnosis of this bone malignancy. Conversely, Tumor cells, benign are not considered a finding of سرطانة العظم, meaning that benign tumor cells do not contribute to the identification or confirmation of this disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 4 -- Community 2857

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 1 -- Community 256
retrieved_docs.content: trauma, surgery, age‑related degeneration, calcific deposition, congenital malformations, and metastatic cancer each generate distinct but often overlapping structural and functional disturbances in the posterior segment, necessitating comprehensive ocular assessment and targeted management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 2564
retrieved_docs.content: Cancer of bone and connective tissue is mapped to ورم خبيث في الفك العلوي, indicating that this malignant condition involves the upper jaw and reflects a neoplastic process affecting both bone and connective tissue structures in that region.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 4 -- Community 2848
retrieved_docs.content: حَمْلٌ بُوقِيّ and ربط البوق are medical concepts that frequently co‑occur, indicating that the presence of حَمْلٌ بُوقِيّ is often associated with the finding or procedure ربط البوق. This consistent association suggests that clinicians should be aware of the link between these two entities, as recognizing one may prompt evaluation for the other, thereby informing diagnosis and guiding appropriate patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 908
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to temporal bone is classified as a malignant neoplasm of temporal bone, indicating that cancerous cells have spread to the temporal bone and are diagnosed as a malignant tumor of that anatomical site. This relationship underscores that the metastatic lesion is considered a specific type of malignant neoplasm affecting the temporal bone, which is relevant for accurate diagnosis, staging, and management in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 2437
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic Lesion and Secondary Lesion are both recognized as diagnostic findings of the disease سرطانة العظم. In patients with سرطانة العظم, the identification of metastatic lesions indicates that malignant cells have spread to bone from another primary site, while secondary lesions represent additional tumor deposits that develop as a consequence of the primary bone malignancy. Recognizing these lesions is essential for accurate diagnosis, staging, and the formulation of appropriate treatment strategies for سرطانة العظم.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2106
retrieved_docs.content: فالج is a general medical condition, and فالج طفلي represents a more specific, narrower form of فالج, indicating the infantile variant of this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 3 -- Community 2623
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic Lesion is recognized as a diagnostic finding associated with سرطانة العظم, indicating that the disease has spread beyond its primary site. In the context of سرطانة العظم, Tumor cells, malignant are identified as the abnormal cellular component that drives the malignancy. Together, the presence of malignant tumor cells and the detection of metastatic lesions provide critical information for diagnosing, staging, and managing سرطانة العظم, guiding therapeutic decisions and prognostic assessment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 4 -- Community 2915
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant Cellular Infiltrate is identified as a pathological finding associated with سرطانة العظم, indicating the presence of infiltrating malignant cells within the affected bone tissue. Likewise, Tumor cells, malignant are recognized as the abnormal cellular component that characterizes سرطانة العظم, reflecting the disease’s neoplastic nature. Together, these findings underscore that both the infiltrative pattern of malignant cells and the presence of malignant tumor cells are key diagnostic features of سرطانة العظم, aiding clinicians in confirming the diagnosis and guiding appropriate management.
